retrieved_docs.title: Fluid Resuscitation -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Additional components for consideration in trauma resuscitation are to minimize the use of crystalloid fluid due to the potential for increasing the inflammatory response. Studies have also shown early use of blood products improve outcomes in trauma patients. [4] The PROPPR (Pragmatic Randomized Optimal Platelet and Plasma Ratios, 2016) study assessed 1:1:1 versus 1:1:2 ratios of plasma to platelets to red blood cells in severely injured patients and found that there were earlier hemorrhage control and decreased deaths secondary to exsanguination in the first 24 hours in the 1:1:1 group. [4] In pediatric patients, fluid resuscitation is indicated in the presence of hemorrhagic shock. An initial bolus of 20 mL/kg of either warmed 0.9% saline or lactated ringers is given. A repeat bolus of 20 mL/kg can be given if there is a transient or no response to initial bolus and then switch to resuscitation with blood products (10 mL/kg). There is no evidence to support permissive hypotension or damage control resuscitation in pediatric patients. [4] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Tactical Damage Control Resuscitation Protocol -- Issues of Concern -- Damage Control Resuscitation
retrieved_docs.content: DCR is based on the premise that coagulopathy in trauma worsens with the disruption of the physiologic balance of whole blood. [18] Prehospital trauma patients in transport are in a critical period defined as the "golden hour," which encompasses the first 60 minutes of the time of injury to definitive hemorrhage control. During this time, opportunities to improve outcomes exist by minimizing physiologic derangements and avoiding the lethal triad of acidosis, coagulopathy, and hypothermia. [19] Military protocols have established a golden hour for delivering combat casualties to an area capable of providing damage control interventions. Subsequent analysis of outcomes of this intervention showed that combat casualties who received blood products or were transferred to centers capable of providing DCR and surgical interventions within an hour of injury had lower mortality. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Tactical Paramedic Lethal Triad -- Issues of Concern -- Acidosis
retrieved_docs.content: Managing acidosis in trauma patients should be focused on correcting the underlying cause of the acidosis, which is typically hemorrhage or organ dysfunction due to poor perfusion. Methods for correcting acidosis include aggressive fluid resuscitation, blood transfusions, and vasoactive medications. These methods improve tissue perfusion and oxygenation of end organs, allowing for a correction of acidosis. Temporary measures such as sodium bicarbonate administration may be employed, but the acidosis persists unless the underlying cause is corrected. In these cases, it is critical to continue monitoring and managing other aspects of the lethal triad, as acidosis can exacerbate coagulopathies and bleeding. Another study revealed an associated increase in mortality in trauma patients with a pH of 7.2 or lower, suggesting that maintaining the patient's pH above 7.2 may be a suitable treatment goal. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Tactical Damage Control Resuscitation Protocol -- Issues of Concern -- Damage Control Resuscitation
retrieved_docs.content: Remote damage control resuscitation (RDCR), as proposed by the Trauma Hemostasis Oxygenation Research (THOR) network, is applied in prehospital settings where access to a hospital is delayed; 60 minutes have elapsed since the injury. This approach is particularly pertinent when immediate hospital treatment is not easily accessible. The THOR network recommends a hypotensive resuscitation strategy for hemorrhagic shock that aims to maintain systolic blood pressure (SBP) at a level lower than normal (approximately 100 mm Hg). This target is still higher than conventional hypotensive resuscitation protocols for EMS systems with short transport times. The approach to hypotensive resuscitation typically involves three key components: delayed fluid administration, restrictive volume administration, and hypotensive targets. This is designed to balance the need for adequate perfusion with the risk of exacerbating hemorrhage. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemorrhagic Shock -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: With a broader understanding of the pathophysiology of hemorrhagic shock, treatment in trauma has expanded from a simple massive transfusion method to a more comprehensive management strategy of “damage control resuscitation.” The concept of damage control resuscitation focuses on permissive hypotension, hemostatic resuscitation, and hemorrhage control to adequately treat the “lethal triad” of coagulopathy, acidosis, and hypothermia that occurs in trauma. The outcomes depend on the cause, patient age, associated comorbidity, and patient response to treatment. [5] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Tactical Damage Control Resuscitation Protocol -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage control is a high priority in trauma patients, and active life-threatening bleeding should be addressed immediately. This stems from prehospital research that indicated risks from advanced airway interventions in the early phase of the management of trauma patients, leading to increased mortality from delayed transport to definitive care and adverse physiologic effects of intubation of patients in hemorrhagic hypovolemic shock. This triggered a culture change in the prehospital management of trauma and led to the prioritization of hemorrhage/circulation over airway in initial management, shifting resuscitation from Airway, Breathing, Circulation (ABCs) to Circulation, Airway, Breathing (CAB) or Massive Hemorrhage, Airway, Respiration, Circulation, Hypothermia Prevention (MARCH) in military settings. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Tactical Damage Control Resuscitation Protocol -- Issues of Concern -- Hemorrhage Control
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with a clear source of life-threatening external hemorrhage, a simultaneous resuscitation approach should be made that immediately addresses hemorrhage control, followed by basic airway interventions and volume resuscitation. The CAB approach to resuscitation should be the standard of care in severely injured trauma patients. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Tactical Damage Control Resuscitation Protocol -- Issues of Concern -- Fluid Resuscitation
retrieved_docs.content: In patients presenting with hypotension and signs suggestive of Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI), especially in the context of multiple injuries and potential internal hemorrhage, hypotensive resuscitation strategies must be tailored to the specific clinical situation. The Western Trauma Association recommends an SBP target of 100 mm Hg for potential TBI cases. In contrast, the Brain Trauma Foundation's 3rd Edition Prehospital Guidelines suggest an SBP above 110 mm Hg in TBI patients, acknowledging a linear relationship between blood pressure and mortality, with higher blood pressure correlating with decreased mortality. They also recognize the need to customize fluid resuscitation strategies in polytrauma patients with TBI and concurrent hemorrhagic shock. Nevertheless, in-hospital guidelines aim for higher blood pressure targets in TBI patients requiring DCR. [33] [34] [35] [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemorrhagic Shock -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: With a broader understanding of the pathophysiology of hemorrhagic shock, treatment in trauma has expanded from a simple massive transfusion method to a more comprehensive management strategy of “damage control resuscitation.” The concept of damage control resuscitation focuses on permissive hypotension, hemostatic resuscitation, and hemorrhage control to adequately treat the “lethal triad” of coagulopathy, acidosis, and hypothermia that occurs in trauma. [12] [13] [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Tactical Damage Control Resuscitation Protocol -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Prehospital EMS services should prioritize blood product resuscitation of hemorrhagic shock in trauma patients over crystalloid resuscitation when available. [18] [19] A tailored approach is necessary for prehospital trauma resuscitation. The potential risks and benefits of exacerbating bleeding versus optimizing hemodynamics should be considered when administering IV fluids, particularly when blood products are unavailable. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Tactical Paramedic Lethal Triad -- Issues of Concern -- Acidosis
retrieved_docs.content: Acidosis is a critical condition in trauma care characterized by a blood pH level of less than 7.35. This condition is typically identified and assessed through a comprehensive analysis of arterial or venous blood gas measurements. In trauma patients, acidosis commonly manifests as lactic acidosis, a direct consequence of tissue hypoxia, which is itself a result of hypovolemia or inadequate tissue perfusion. Other concurrent conditions, such as hypothermia and coagulopathy, often intensify the severity and impact of acidosis in patients. Hypothermia can impair metabolic processes, further aggravating the body's acid-base imbalance. Similarly, coagulopathy can also contribute to the complexity and severity of acidosis. This interplay between acidosis, hypothermia, and coagulopathy in trauma patients creates a challenging clinical scenario, necessitating careful and prompt management to mitigate their combined detrimental effects on patient outcomes. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Fluid Resuscitation -- Indications -- Trauma
retrieved_docs.content: Two major types of fluids used for resuscitation are colloids which specifically expand the intravascular volume and crystalloids, which briefly expand the intravascular volume and quickly re-distribute into the interstitial compartment. For resuscitation, crystalloids are given as 1- to 2-liter bolus in patients with hemorrhagic shock. However, recent studies using colloids favor permissive hypotension. For example, in patients with penetrating trauma, aggressive fluid resuscitation may exacerbate bleeding, so the emphasis is on administering small boluses of fluid (250mL) allowing a low systolic blood pressure equal to  90 mm Hg or mean blood pressure equal to 50 mm Hg until one achieves sustained hemorrhagic control. This strategy has been shown to improve survival and reduce the amount of fluid replaced.

retrieved_docs.title: Liver Trauma -- Treatment / Management -- Operative Management
retrieved_docs.content: Hemodynamically unstable patients, not responsive to resuscitation, should go directly from the trauma bay to the operating room for laparotomy. In addition, patients with peritoneal signs should go to the operating room. Patients that fail non-operative therapy must undergo laparotomy. Damage control laparotomy principles should be followed in the unstable patient undergoing laparotomy. This includes first controlling hemorrhage, then controlling gastrointestinal (GI) contamination. The abdomen should be packed in all 4 quadrants, allowing for injuries to be localized and the anesthesia team to resuscitate the patient. The “lethal triad” of coagulopathy, acidosis, and hypothermia should trigger a damage control approach.

retrieved_docs.title: Penetrating Abdominal Trauma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Quickly triaging those with penetrating abdominal trauma using Advanced Trauma Life Support as designed by the American College of Surgeons is highly important. The objectives are to address bleeding and contamination, correct temperature, acidosis, and coagulopathy. [21] [37] Trauma injury scales assist with triage, and initial measures include rewarming, resuscitation that includes electrolyte repletion and transfusion, and use of permissive hypotension. [37] [38]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Tactical Damage Control Resuscitation Protocol -- Issues of Concern -- Fluid Resuscitation
retrieved_docs.content: Most civilian prehospital EMS services do not have access to blood products for resuscitation. While crystalloid is less than ideal for the resuscitation of hemorrhage, it is frequently the only product available. Some studies have found that allowing for permissive hypotension versus crystalloid resuscitation (no IV fluids versus standard of care) has a mortality benefit, with those who received prehospital crystalloid resuscitation presenting with greater degrees of coagulopathy and anemia than those managed conservatively. [19] Resuscitation protocols of controlled 250 mL crystalloid boluses for profound hypotension (SBP <70 mmHg or absent radial pulse), when compared with liberal crystalloid resuscitation, show early mortality benefits in some patients with hypotension secondary to trauma. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Geriatric Head Injury -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Blood pressure management is required to prevent hypotension.  Normal saline is preferred to achieve euvolemia when compared to dextrose or balanced crystalloid solutions such as lactated Ringer's solution in cases of traumatic brain injury to prevent cerebral edema. [13] Airway management, such as intubation, may be necessary for airway protection and to prevent hypoxia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: irrigating solutions and electrolyte‑balancing formulations are organized within a unified pharmacological framework, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate intravenous therapies for fluid resuscitation, nutrition, electrolyte management, antifibrinolytic treatment and metabolic modulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 165
retrieved_docs.content: of falls and traffic accidents, highlighting the anatomical, pathological and procedural relationships that are essential for comprehensive assessment, diagnosis and management of patients with polytrauma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2566
retrieved_docs.content: Nonmechanical method of resuscitation encompasses manual techniques for reviving patients, and its specific child concept is إنعاش قلبي رئوي (cardiopulmonary resuscitation), which represents the hands‑on approach used to restore breathing and circulation without reliance on mechanical devices.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific form of فقر الدم (anemia) that arises rapidly after substantial blood loss, leading to a sudden decrease in circulating red blood cells and hemoglobin levels. As a subtype of anemia, it shares the fundamental characteristic of reduced oxygen‑carrying capacity of the blood, but its acute onset distinguishes it from chronic or nutritional anemias. Prompt recognition and treatment of acute posthemorrhagic anemia are essential in patient care, as rapid restoration of intravascular volume and red cell mass—through fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, and control of the bleeding source—are required to prevent tissue hypoxia, organ dysfunction, and hemodynamic instability. Understanding that acute posthemorrhagic anemia falls under the broader category of فقر الدم helps clinicians classify the condition, anticipate its pathophysiology, and apply appropriate therapeutic strategies within the overall framework of anemia management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 188
retrieved_docs.content: Together, these interrelated entities and hierarchies provide a comprehensive framework that aligns preventive measures, micronutrient support, emergency resuscitation, and a spectrum of minimally invasive to major therapeutic interventions, facilitating precise documentation, coding, and patient‑centered care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 58
retrieved_docs.content: therapies, vascular and orthopedic surgical categories, nutritional and resuscitative modalities, and diagnostic imaging, providing a structured view of how diverse medical procedures are classified, interrelated, and applied in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 481
retrieved_docs.content: that suffocation leads to reduced blood oxygen levels, and syncope is classified as a subtype of loss of consciousness (فقدان الوعي). Collectively, the knowledge base emphasizes how genetic, infectious, hematologic, respiratory, neurological, and psychiatric factors intersect with critical outcomes such as respiratory failure, hypoxemia, syncope, suffocation, femoral neck fracture, and death, and underscores the importance of both therapeutic interventions and prevention‑control strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 257
retrieved_docs.content: upper GI hemorrhage. Collectively, these interrelated entities demonstrate that disturbances in fluid volume, electrolyte concentrations, acid‑base balance, and glucose homeostasis are central to the pathophysiology of acute renal failure, pancreatitis, hepatic injury, urinary infections, respiratory disease, and gastrointestinal bleeding, guiding clinicians toward comprehensive assessment and coordinated management of patients with overlapping metabolic and organ‑specific abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: cardioplegic electrolyte additives are integrated within a structured pharmacologic framework that supports fluid‑electrolyte management, organ protection, and therapeutic interventions in cardiac surgical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2105
retrieved_docs.content: Subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness is associated with إصابة داخلية (internal injury), indicating that this form of hemorrhagic brain trauma often co‑occurs with internal injuries and should be evaluated together in clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1938
retrieved_docs.content: Cortisol succinate is identified as a potential therapeutic agent for the management of shock. Shock, a life‑threatening circulatory failure, is classified in medical terminology as “صدمة إنتانيّة” (infectious shock) and also as “صَدْمَة قَلْبِيَّة المَنْشَأ” (cardiogenic shock), reflecting its distinct etiologic subtypes. Recognizing that cortisol succinate may be used to treat these severe shock states underscores its relevance in acute care settings where rapid reversal of hemodynamic collapse is essential.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2133
retrieved_docs.content: Fenylefrin, a potent α‑adrenergic agonist commonly used as a vasopressor, may be employed in the treatment of صدمة إنتانيّة (septic shock). By inducing peripheral vasoconstriction, fenylefrin can help raise systemic vascular resistance and improve arterial blood pressure in patients whose circulatory collapse is driven by overwhelming infection. Its use in septic shock is typically considered when rapid restoration of hemodynamic stability is required, often in conjunction with fluid resuscitation and other supportive measures. Proper dosing and monitoring are essential to balance the benefits of increased perfusion pressure against the risk of excessive vasoconstriction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2453
retrieved_docs.content: Other nonoperative procedures include the subcategory Other miscellaneous procedures. Other miscellaneous procedures have a broader relationship to إنعاش قلبي رئوي (cardiopulmonary resuscitation), indicating that CPR is considered part of these miscellaneous, non‑operative interventions. In addition, إنعاش قلبي رئوي is classified as a child of the Nonmechanical method of resuscitation, linking it to resuscitation techniques that do not rely on mechanical devices. Consequently, cardiopulmonary resuscitation is both a nonmechanical resuscitation method and an element of the broader group of other miscellaneous procedures within the overall framework of other nonoperative procedures, clarifying its place in procedural taxonomy for clinical documentation and patient care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1294
retrieved_docs.content: Metaraminol and cortisol succinate are both identified as potential therapeutic agents for the management of shock, indicating that each may be used to treat this life‑threatening condition. Their inclusion as possible treatments highlights the importance of having multiple pharmacologic options when addressing the hemodynamic instability characteristic of shock, thereby supporting tailored patient care and improving the likelihood of stabilizing circulatory function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2237
retrieved_docs.content: Blood substitutes and perfusion solutions constitute a specific category of intravenous (IV) solutions that are administered parenterally to deliver fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients. As a subclass of IV solutions used for parenteral administration, these products are formulated to replace or augment blood components, provide rapid volume expansion, and supply essential nutrients when oral intake is not possible. They play a critical role in clinical settings such as fluid resuscitation for hypovolemia, management of blood loss, and provision of nutritional support for patients who require intravenous therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 813
retrieved_docs.content: Internal injury of chest is recognized as a traumatic event and functions as a cause of subsequent medical complications, highlighting its role as an initiating injury in clinical assessments and patient care.
